coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots with high quantum yield and their application in construction of fluorescence-labeled shape memory nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Pei
Zhu, Ziqi
Li, Xinghui
Zhang, Tao
Zhang, Wei
Chen, Minzhi
Zhou, Xiaoyan
coredata.dc:description: Synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) with efficient long-wavelength emissions (i.e., yellow-to red light) generally suffer from sophisticated approaches, time-consuming process, harsh conditions, and requirement of organic solvent; also, a further limitation of the resulting CDs is relatively low quantum yield (QY) in aqueous solution. Herein, novel yellow emitting CDs (Y-CDs) with a considerable QY of 62.8% were synthesized from a precursor comprising resorcinol and o-phenylenediamine via a facile microwave method. To probe the fluorescence mechanism, another typical resorcinol-derived CDs using ethylenediamine as dopant were fabricated as well, showing strong green emission with an absolute QY of 60.6%. Spectroscopic and structural characterizations indicated that the distinct redshift of green to yellow emission depended on the dimension of conjugated sp2-domain and the content of graphitic N heavily, while the excellent QY was highly related to the low proportion of defective sp2 carbon cluster and high nitrogen content within CDs. Moreover, the Y-CDs were confirmed to be capable of introducing additional crosslinking points in poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) polymer, which resulted in the Y-CDs-contained nanocomposite behaving superior and tunable water-induced shape recovery performances. Importantly, since being labeled with long-wavelength emission, the responsiveness of PVA/Y-CDs composite will contribute to its versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Yellow emission
Fluorescence mechanism
Nanocomposite
Shape recovery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549 ), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation ( BK20161524 ), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381 ), the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX17_839 ), the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of Y-CDs (a) and G-CDs (b) under excitation light of different wavelengths, the insert photos are the corresponding CDs aqueous solution under UV light. 3D fluorescence plots of Y-CDs (c) and G-CDs (d).

Absorption behavior (blue curve), fluorescence emission (red curve), and fluorescence excitation (yellow curve) of Y-CDs (a) and G-CDs (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
TEM image and HRTEM image of Y-CDs (a–b), and G-CDs (c–d).
Histograms of the particle size distribution of Y-CDs (a), and G-CDs (b).
XPS full survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS of the C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra of the Y-CDs (a), and G-CDs (b).

(a) Transmittance of pure PVA film and PVA/Y-CDs composite film. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of PVA/Y-CDs composite excited from 360 to 480 nm. (c) Normalized optimal emission spectrum of PVA/Y-CDs composite and Y-CDs aqueous solution.

(a) water-induced shape memory behavior of the quatrefoil shaped sample under 365 nm UV light. (b) Shape recovery ratio of PVA/Y-CDs nanocomposite under different water pH conditions. (c) Shape recovery ratio of pure PVA in water. (d) Water-induced shape memory behavior of PVA/Y-CDs nanocomposite strip under a UV lamp in dark environment, and (e) recovery process of deformed PVA strip in natural light.

DMA of PVA/Y-CDs composite and pure PVA, (a) Storage modulus as a function of temperature; (b) tangent delta as a function of temperature. (c) Comparison of XRD spectra of PVA/Y-CDs composite and pure PVA. (d) FTIR spectra of PVA/Y-CDs composite and pure PVA showing the changing of O–H stretching vibration.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of yellow emissive carbon dots with high quantum yield and their application in construction of fluorescence-labeled shape memory nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fast-growing Tree & Agro-fibre Materials Engineering Center, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) with efficient long-wavelength emissions (i.e., yellow-to red light) generally suffer from sophisticated approaches, time-consuming process, harsh conditions, and requirement of organic solvent; also, a further limitation of the resulting CDs is relatively low quantum yield (QY) in aqueous solution. Herein, novel yellow emitting CDs (Y-CDs) with a considerable QY of 62.8% were synthesized from a precursor comprising resorcinol and o-phenylenediamine via a facile microwave method. To probe the fluorescence mechanism, another typical resorcinol-derived CDs using ethylenediamine as dopant were fabricated as well, showing strong green emission with an absolute QY of 60.6%. Spectroscopic and structural characterizations indicated that the distinct redshift of green to yellow emission depended on the dimension of conjugated sp2-domain and the content of graphitic N heavily, while the excellent QY was highly related to the low proportion of defective sp2 carbon cluster and high nitrogen content within CDs. Moreover, the Y-CDs were confirmed to be capable of introducing additional crosslinking points in poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) polymer, which resulted in the Y-CDs-contained nanocomposite behaving superior and tunable water-induced shape recovery performances. Importantly, since being labeled with long-wavelength emission, the responsiveness of PVA/Y-CDs composite will contribute to its versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Emerging from carbon materials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn heightened attention in past decades because of their superior optical and chemical properties. Compared with the conventional fluorescent materials, such as semiconductor quantum dots, organic dyes and rare-earth phosphors, most of CDs reported so far mainly consisted of non-toxic C, O and N elements, and are thus superior in the aspects of green synthesis, outstanding photoluminescence (PL) performances, good water solubility, ease of functionalization, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Based on these intrinsic characteristics, CDs have demonstrated a variety of potential applications, such as bioimaging [1,2], sensing [3–5], anti-counterfeit printing [6,7], photocatalysis [8–10], and optoelectronic devices [11,12]. Although a great number of synthesis strategies have been developed to fabricate CDs and improve their PL quantum yield (QY), most of known CDs exhibit blue- or green-biased emission behavior under excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light. Specifically, the PL spectra of these CDs likewise follows similar trend in which the optimal emission peaks are located in the blue and green light region, while further increasing excitation wavelength generally lead to the attenuation of PL intensities. Such a limiting factor, which expressed as small penetration depth, monotonous emissive light and inferior anti-interference property for most currently available CDs, unquestionably restrict their wider applications, particularly in the biology-related use, light-emitting devices, and information encryption. Therefore, synthesizing CDs that emit luminescence with maxima in the long-wavelength region via a facile approach, and achieving their high PL efficiency are still big challenges, because, unlike to the semiconductor quantum dots, the tunable emission of CDs can be hardly achieved by tailoring the particle size alone.

Attempts at achieving long-wavelength emissions of CDs have, to date, only been reported in few publications. In view of these researches, the multi-colored emissions in CDs could be realized via heteroatom doping, size control, solvatochromic effect, concentration-dependent effect, and surface functionalization [13–17]. In addition, valuable utilization in bioimaging [18], fingerprint information recognition [19], promoting photosynthesis [20], and constructing light emitting diodes [21,22] were also proposed on the basis of these charming CDs. Furthermore, substantial efforts in these previous works were made to probe the underlying reasons for the long-wavelength emissions, and experimental investigations have illustrated that the multiple PL properties strongly depend on the complicated chemical structures of CDs, involving conjugated sp2-domain, particle size, graphitization degree, heteroatom doping effect, surface-contained functional groups, etc. [1,11,18,21,23]. Nonetheless, these synthesized CDs with emission wavelength beyond the green light region generally suffer from several limitations: (1) the hydrothermal synthetic process is time- and energy-consuming; (2) the sophisticated separation or purification is always necessary; (4) the tunable fluorescence is commonly realized via the solvothermal route or with the aid of dissolving CDs into organic solvent; (3) the QYs determined in water is relatively low compared with the typical blue emissive CDs. Motivated by these issues, efforts should still be given to the significant work in this area for obtaining long-wavelength emissive CDs with high QYs, as well as exploring their novel application.

As well known, shape memory polymers (SMPs) are able to experience large-scale shape changes with quick response upon exposing to a specific stimulus such as heat, light, solvents, electric, and magnetic fields. Structurally, stable cross-linked networks and switching segments are the two prerequisites for the construction of SMPs with shape recovery behavior [24]. Over the past few years, numerous SMP composites have been developed and exhibited their different stimuli-responsive performances, among these developed polymer-based SMPs, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as polymer matrix have caught increasing attention due to their larruping advantages, including nontoxic nature, easy processable, bio-compatibility and good mechanical properties. More importantly, PVA-based SMPs provide large possibilities to realize water-induced shape behavior, because water stimulus is much easier and milder for versatile utilizations. Combining the above merits, the function feature of PVA-based SMPs is more suitable to meet the requirement of many advanced applications, especially for biomedical engineering. In general, to improve the shape recovery properties of PVA-based SMPs, increasing researches have pointed out that the formation of extra hydrogen bonding interactions on polymer chains by immobilizing appropriate nanofillers in PVA matrix is an efficient way. In this regard, various fillers have been developed, such as polyaniline fibres [25], carboxymethyl cellulose sodium [26], aluminum oxide [27], and graphene oxide [28]. Commonly, for the PVA-based SMPs, the H-bond based crosslinking network induced by nanofillers is susceptive to water molecules, and become weak in stiffness and mechanical stability with the increase of water content in SMP composite, which contribute more to the shape recovery of the SMPs. Such behavior has been extensively investigated in previous reports, and the intrinsic reasons are highly related to the plasticizing effect of water and the competitive hydrogen bonding interactions within the crosslinking structure of SMPs [28,29]. However, it should be noted that the nonluminous characteristic of the water-responsive SMPs suffers difficulty in tracking their recovery behaviors in dark environment, and therefore limiting their further development, in particular for their utilization in organism tissue. Benefiting from several nanometers in size, strong fluorescence, surface-contained multiple functional groups and excellent solvent dispensability, CDs are promising nanofillers that can be used to construct PVA-based SMPs featuring with stable fluorescence. Inspired by this charming function, a few studies have reported on the potential benefits of water-induced shape memory of PVA/CDs composite [29,30]. Despite these great efforts, the blue emission arising from these constructed PVA/CDs composites limit their proposed utilization in biomedicine field, because the biological matrix always behaves blue autofluorescence simultaneously once the CDs-contained SMPs was monitored using excitation light, such phenomenon is adverse to tracing the shape recovery when the constructed fluorescent SMPs are used in biological system. Thus, it is of great and profound significant to construct water activated SMPs with long-wavelength emission.

In this research, we synthesized yellow emitting CDs (Y-CDs) from a precursor comprising resorcinol and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) via a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method within just 10 min. The absolute PLQY of Y-CDs reached a level up to as high as 62.76%, which would be comparable to most of blue- or green-emitting CDs, and was much greater than the long-wavelength emissive CDs synthesized so far. Furthermore, the PL emission mechanism for these resultant nanostructures was probed by comparatively investigating the structure, morphology, and chemical composition. For expanding the application of CDs, the synthesized Y-CDs were incorporated into PVA matrix to achieve functionalized nanocomposite. Thanks to the formation of distinct H-bond based crosslinking network induced by Y-CDs, the Y-CDs-contained PVA film demonstrated excellent water-induced shape recovery behavior, accompanied with yellow fluorescence monitoring in dark environment. Additionally, this water-induced SMP constructed on the basis of Y-CDs and PVA showed tunable responsiveness by changing the environmental pH value. Such inherent biological stimuli (water and pH), which together with the traced long-wavelength fluorescence, will endow the PVA/Y-CDs SMP with diverse biomedical application potential, such as smart biosensor, stimuli-responsive drug-release system and novel medical devices.

Resorcinol (≥99%), oPD (≥99%), and PVA (M.W. = 145,000) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), HCl and NaOH were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Reagent grade of ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Shanghai Hansi Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the whole experiments.

The commercially available microwave instrument used in our experiment was produced by Chemistry Electronic Microwave Company, (DISCOVER SP, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images was captured by a JEM-2100 UHR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was investigated on a Vertex 80V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with the scanning range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The CDs solid samples were measured by the KBr tablet method, while the PVA/CDs composites were tested using attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. Surface charge measurement was performed on a ZEN3600 Zeta Plus (Malvern, UK) instrument with a He–Ne laser source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an AXIS Ultra DLD electron spectrometer (Shimadzu, UK) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. UV–vis absorption was performed using a Lambda 950 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The PL performances were investigated by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer manufactured by Hitachi, both the excitation and emission slits were 10 nm. Absolute PLQY of CDs samples were measured by using an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer. Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were measured using a FLS1000 time-correlated single-photon counting system (Edinburgh, UK). Wide-angle X-ray diffractograms (XRD) analysis was realized by a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer adopting Cu radiation with Kα wavelength of 0.1541 nm. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was performed on a DMA 242E (NETZSCH, Germany), the operating frequency was 1 Hz, and the test temperature ranged from −40 to 120 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1.

In a typical procedure, 2 mmol resorcinol (220 mg), 2 mmol oPD (216 mg), and 200 μL HCl were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water for the synthesis of Y-CDs, the mixture was then transferred into a 30 mL reaction tube, the glass tube containing starting solution was heated to 180 °C within tens of seconds and kept for an additional 10 min. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the reaction mixture was neutralized carefully by NaOH solution. Subsequently, the resulting suspension was filtered by a PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.22 μm to remove possible large particles, and then the filtered solution subjected to dialysis (MWCO 800 Da) against ultrapure water for 48 h. This purified Y-CDs aqueous solution was collected for further use; also, the solid Y-CDs sample was obtained as lightweight powder after freeze-drying. To probe the possible PL mechanism, 2 mmol EDA (about 133 μL) was used to instead of oPD for preparing another typical nitrogen-doped CDs, while the synthesis conditions were same as the preparation of Y-CDs. Because the green fluorescence can be observed from these resultant CDs under a 365 nm UV lamp, this type of CDs synthesized using EDA as nitrogen dopant was labeled as G-CDs.

In brief, 2 g of PVA powder was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, this mixture suffered magnetic stirring at 90 °C for 2 h, then the obtained PVA solution was mixed with 25 mg of Y-CDs under continuous stirring for 1 h to ensure the thorough mixing of PVA and Y-CDs. Subsequently, the PVA/Y-CDs mixture was degassed using ultrasound for 30 min to remove the air bubbles and then poured into a watch glass. Finally, the sample was dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven until a constant weight was reached. Prior to use, the prepared PVA/Y-CDs composite films were sealed in a desiccator to prevent from air moisture.

A bend-recovery test was performed to evaluate the shape recovery performance of PVA/Y-CDs composite in water. The composite film was cut into straight strip (40 mm length, 5 mm width, and 0.2 mm thickness) and then bent to an angle (θ 0) close to 180 at 80 °C. After that, the temporary angle (θ i) generating from slight deformation was fixed by external force during cooling. Finally, the V-shape specimen was immersed into water at room temperature (25 °C), and the change of bending angle (θ f) with the prolongation of immersion time was recorded. The shape recovery ratio (R r) of the specimen was expressed as (θ i − θ f)/θ i. For monitoring in real-time, the recovery behavior of specimen was captured using a digital camera.

Benefiting from many advantages, such as environmentally friendly, simple in operation and easy to implement, hydrothermal processing was widely used for the preparation of nanomaterials over the past several decades and also deemed as an efficient way for the sustainable development of CDs [31–34]. In addition, considering that microwave heating does not only reduce the reaction time by several orders of magnitude, but also can suppress side reactions, thus playing an important role in synthetic chemistry. Therefore, combing the benefits of hydrothermal approach and microwave technology will provide a green and rapid synthetic pathway for the production of CDs with long-wavelength emissions. As an optimal example, Y-CDs were prepared facilely through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method within just 10 min, using the diluted HCl solution of resorcinol and oPD as starting materials. It should be mentioned that the absence of HCl in the starting solution led to the aggregation of resultant product which emitted weak fluorescence under UV lamp. Hence, the trace amount of HCl can act as reaction promoter in solvent to facilitate the dehydration of the precursors, and could be also conducive to the polymerization and carbonization of the molecule fragments, thus resulting in the formation of compact carbon cores with stable luminescence centers. The possible promotion effect demonstrated above endowed the Y-CDs with a relatively bright fluorescence and high production yield. In the control experiment. Note that the mole number of amino groups in EDA molecule was almost the same as that contained within oPD, the added EDA, mixing with the HCl solution of resorcinol was employed as the nitrogen precursor of G-CDs.

Although an opportunity for doping same nitrogen content was provided, the resultant Y-CDs and G-CDs demonstrated a distinct difference in emission behaviors. As shown in Fig. 1 , panel a illustrates that the Y-CDs were characterized by a typical excitation-independent PL performance. Specifically, as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 480 nm, the emission band peaked at 555 nm was insusceptible to this variation, while the PL intensities increased firstly and decreased later. The excitation independency of emission behavior could be mainly attributed to the homogeneous microstructure and surface chemical states of the Y-CDs. Notably, despite pretty low PL intensity, CDs synthesized from precursor comprising resorcinol and oPD without adding HCl showed a dominating emission light at wavelength of about 555 nm (Fig. S1), which was the same as the main emission arising from Y-CDs. Remarkably, the excitation at shorter wavelength led to the emergence of shoulder peak in blue-light region; also, this weak emission showed excitation-dependent behavior which was basically similar to those widely observed in most of blue emissive CDs containing carbon topological defects [35]. Thus, it is reasonable to deduce that HCl in the precursor of Y-CDs was beneficial to carbonizing intermediate products or structures. Consider this possible reason, we concluded that the incorporation of HCl was not the key factor of resulting yellow fluorescence, but played a critical role in synthesizing CDs with enhanced fluorescence and production yield. As for the G-CDs, the emission behavior was examined at excitation wavelength ranging from 360 to 480 nm. Clearly, there were double emission bands presented in the emission spectra obtained at shorter excitation wavelengths (360–400 nm), taking the case of 360 excitation as an example, the emission peak of shoulder band was located at 435 nm, while the main emission peak can be observed at 519 nm. With the further increase of excitation wavelength, the shoulder peak disappeared gradually, and the main emission exhibited slight redshift in the wavelength range of 519–528 nm, accompanied by the sharply increased PL intensity (Fig. 1b). Moreover, both the Y-CDs and G-CDs aqueous solution displayed a single narrow emission center in 3D fluorescence plots. For the Y-CDs, the maximum emission center was located at 555 nm with an excitation wavelength of 398 nm (Fig. 1c), while the optimal excitation and emission of G-CDs were observed at 485 nm and 529 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d). Fig. S2 of the supporting information illustrates the pH-dependent PL performances of the two CDs samples, it is apparent that both of the CDs samples demonstrated their weak fluorescence under acid condition. For the low-dimensional materials like CDs, doping H+ was equivalent to injecting holes into their structure, leading to change that the photogenerated electrons can be easily captured by these extra holes [36,37]. As a consequence, the efficient radiative recombination was weakened, which ultimately lowered PL intensities of the synthesized CDs. Furthermore, measuring by an integrating sphere under 400 nm excitation, the Y-CDs exhibited the highest QY of 62.8% among the reported CDs with long-wavelength emission in water (Fig. S3). In addition, the QY of 60.6% was also observed for G-CDs (Fig. S4), which was also superior over most of blue- or green-emitting CDs.

Fig. 2 shows a comparison of absorption, PL excitation (PLE) spectra, and PL spectra of the two types of CDs samples. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Y-CDs exhibited an obvious peak at about 276 nm, which is usually assigned to the π–π∗ transition occurred in sp2 carbogenic domains [38,39]. Besides, in the lower energy region, a weak absorption band at 400–500 nm was also distinct, which could be aroused by the defect states within CDs structures or the large sized conjugated sp2-domain in the nitrogen-rich multilayer graphene-like cores [19,21,38]. As seen in Fig. 2a, it is clear that the PLE spectrum of Y-CDs was partially overlapped with the absorption curve in the visible region, indicating that the defect states transition at the core edge could also contribute to the yellow fluorescence of Y-CDs. Significantly, the slight overlap between absorption and emission spectra in the long-wavelength region indicated that Y-CDs can hardly absorb some part of the PL emission generating from luminescent centers, and was thus bestowed with efficient fluorescence emission [21]. Unlike the Y-CDs, the absorption behavior of G-CDs only demonstrated π–π∗ transition with a characteristic peak at around 272 nm, and showed no overlapped phenomena with emission and excitation spectrum (Fig. 2b). The fluorescence decay dynamics for Y-CDs and G-CDs were also measured to understand the electronic transition process in molecular level. As shown in Fig. S5, both the PL decay spectra of the Y-CDs and G-CDs in aqueous solution can be well fitted with a mono-exponential function, the lifetime component was 1.55 ns for Y-CDs, and 2.28 ns for G-CDs. Based on the pioneers’ research, the mono-exponential decay kinetics imply that the two typical CDs underwent similar luminescence processes and there was only one dominating radiative transition channel accounting for their PL emissions [40,41].

The morphological observation was performed firstly to understand the difference in PL properties of Y-CDs and G-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 it can be clearly seen from the TEM image that the Y-CDs were nearly spherical with excellent dispersibility (Fig. 3a). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images displayed well-resolved lattice fringes (Fig. 3b), the determined interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm was close to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes, suggesting the crystalline nature of Y-CDs. In addition, as observed from TEM image of the G-CDs (Fig. 3c), these nanostructures also had a uniform dispersion but exhibited relatively small particle size compared with Y-CDs. Clearly, attributed to the same carbon source and synthesis strategy, the resultant G-CDs was also featured with same crystalline nature as that for Y-CDs (Fig. 3d), thus demonstrating their same multilayer graphene-like core structures. The particle size distribution of each CDs sample was investigated as judged from 100 individual particles, the corresponding histograms are displayed in Fig. 4 , panel a illustrates that the dimension of Y-CDs ranged from 3 to 10 nm with an average diameter of 6.1 ± 1.2 nm, In contrast, the G-CDs had a size distribution in the region of 1.5–5 nm and average diameter of 3.1 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 4b). Although the particle size distribution of Y-CDs and G-CDs were both relatively wide, it is note worth that most of Y-CDs had a uniform diameter in a small range of 5–7 nm, while the dimension of large proportion of G-CDs was concentrated below 4 nm. The larger particle sizes of Y-CDs could be resulted from the large sp2-domain that was formed due to the intense reaction of resorcinol and oPD in HCl solution. Thus, it is reasonable to conclude that the sp2-domain take the dominant place within the core structure and further help to tailor the emission properties of CDs.

The surface chemical species of the synthesized nanostructures were explored by FTIR assignment. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the similar absorption bands can be observed in the FTIR spectra of Y-CDs and G-CDs, revealing little difference in their surface-contained functional groups. The characteristic stretching vibration bands of O–H, N–H, and C–H were observed at 3421, 3238, and 2896 cm−1, respectively. Note that the emergence of absorption peak at 2595 cm−1 should be attributed to the –SH vibrations [42,43], the detected S element in CDs could come from sulfur-containing impurities in industrial HCl reagent. Moreover, the sharp absorption peaks at 1624 and 1506 cm−1 corresponded to the CN and CC stretching vibrations [11,13,16], it is worthy of note that the peak intensity of CC bond within Y-CDs was much higher than that within G-CDs, indicating that the Y-CDs contained relatively large sized conjugated sp2-domain in their nanostructures. Meanwhile, the bands at 1410 and 1284 cm−1 were the characteristics of C–N and aromatic C–NH stretching vibrations [44], while the typical peak located at 1149 cm−1 was related to the C–O bond. In addition, the absorption band at 750 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of Y-CDs was much more pronounced, which could be correlate with the C–Cl groups [45], suggesting the covalent bonding of chlorine atoms in the carbon skeleton of Y-CDs. As for the G-CDs, the characteristic absorption peak of C–Cl bonds was obscure for the G-CDs because their content could be at a low level. We speculated that HCl could react with oPD through electrophilic reaction during hydrothermal process, which facilitates Cl doping within the structure of Y-CDs in some extent. Measurement of Zeta potential show that the Y- and G-CDs had negative surface potentials of −12.8 and −0.19 eV, respectively. the negatively charged properties of the two typical CD particles and their significant difference could be dominated or affected by Cl-containing groups and amino groups. In spite of the distinction in Cl-related chemical moieties, the Y- and G-CDs synthesized from resorcinol coupling with oPD or EDA were mainly composed of polyaromatic structures as well as abundant N-related groups.

XPS findings were used to further examine the chemical composition of CDs samples for detailed comparisons, as shown in Fig. 5 ; also, the relative content of each chemical bond within the CDs’ structures was also extracted, as tabulated in Table S1. The full spectra of Y- and G-CDs exhibited five distinct peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), O1s (533 eV), Cl2s (269 eV), and Cl2p (198 eV), indicating their same element composition. Additionally, the chemical composition of the Y-CDs was 69.8% C, 15.8% N, 6.2% O, and 8.2% Cl, while for the G-CDs, the atomic percentage was 60.7, 15.1, 11.9, and 12.3% for C, N, O and Cl, respectively. Further, the O to C ratio for Y-CDs and G-CDs was determined to be 8.88% and 19.60%, respectively, suggesting a relatively high degree of graphitization in Y-CDs. By contrast, there was no obvious change can be found for N/C ratio, although the N precursors of Y-CDs and G-CDs were different. The above data indicates that the Y- and G-CDs incorporated almost the same amount of nitrogen element within their structures. In the high-resolution of C1s spectra, the characteristic peaks of CC/C–C (284.5 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), and C–O (286.4 eV) can be found in both of the two CDs samples. Particularly, the CC/C–C moieties within Y-CDs reached up to 33.67%, which is relatively high compared with 19.86% of G-CDs, thus indicating that the Y-CDs could contain much more aromatic rings. For O1s, XPS spectra can be converted into CO (531.8 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (532.7 eV), and C–O (533.5 eV) groups [20]. Although the N content in Y- and G-CDs were closely similar, several distinctions were presented in the N-related chemical forms. In detail, The N1s band of Y-CDs was deconvoluted into three distinct binding energy peaks at 399.4, 400.4 and 401.8 eV, which are assigned to the pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and amino N, respectively [18,46]. The XPS N1s spectrum of G-CDs also indicated the presence of pyrrolic N and amino N, but without diagnostic signal of graphitic N occurring from G-CDs, thus confirming the shortage of this chemical species in G-CDs. Besides, it is worthy to note here that there was relatively more pyrrolic N can be found in Y-CDs than in G-CDs, as illustrate in Table S1. Since the pyrrolic N can contribute to the π-conjugated system with a pair of π-electrons, the large amount of pyrrolic N within Y-CDs could help to form more efficient conjugated aromatic π systems [1], which in turn reveals that the Y-CDs contained much larger sp2-domain than G-CDs. In a nutshell, for the synthesized Y-CDs, the sp2 carbon content was at a high level, and the graphitic N was also formed due likely to the participation of oPD in the CDs synthesis reaction. From several previous investigations, the extended efficient conjugation length which commonly expressed as increased particle size is likely to narrow the band gap resulting from π electron delocalization within the sp2 domain [1,21,47]. According to Holá and co-workers’ research, graphitic N within the carbon lattice of CDs can donate excess electrons into the unoccupied π∗ orbitals of a conjugated system, resulting in the visible absorption and red-shift of emission [48]. Inspired from these results, it is reasonable to conclude that the distinct excitation-independent yellow emission of Y-CDs was contributed from both the large conjugated sp2-domain and graphitic N within the carbogenic cores. In the case of G-CDs, their excitonic absorption was located in only the UV region, which could be closely correlated with the relatively small sized sp2-domain as well as the absence of graphitic N; also, such behavior indicated that the luminescent centers of G-CDs, where fluorescence was generated from the recombination of electrons and holes, mainly associated with the π-conjugated system. Moreover, several defect states induced by N-related functionalities or increased O-containing groups could also existed within the electronic structure of G-CDs, but had weak capacity to generate efficient electron transitions because the sp2-domain had dominating effect on the electronic properties. Thus, the trivial defect states of GY-CDs gave rise to shoulder emission at blue light region, while the small sized conjugated sp2-domain was more likely to be responsible for the green fluorescence.

Based on the above-mentioned analysis, the formation of Y-CDs with high QY was tentatively proposed. In our case, with the assistance of microwave irradiation, the precursor underwent polymerization initially, which resulted in the production of numerous intermediate aromatic compounds including complete sp2-hybridized carbon and irregular carbon frameworks with carbon vacancy. As the reaction proceeds, intermolecular dehydration was happened under the harsh condition, and was also accelerated due to the introduction HCl in starting solution, which further resulted in the nucleation of CDs, accompanied with efficient carbonization. Since the similar molecular fragments took part in the intense reactions, the resulting nuclei grow uniformly and isotropically toward the carbon cores. As a result, most of vacancy in carbon frameworks was eliminated, and the large sp2-domain took the dominant place within the core part of CDs. During the reaction, a great of N atoms was doped within the sp2 clusters, and also connected on the edge of graphitic layers simultaneously.

It has been known that introducing additional cross-linked points in PVA matrix could improve the shape memory performances of PVA using water as stimulus. Inspired by the easy formation of hydrogen bonding interactions with the PVA chains, the Y-CDs with surface-contained multiple functional groups were employed as nanofillers to construct PVA/Y-CDs nanocomposite with fluorescence-labeled shape recovery behavior. Prior to evaluating water-induced shape memory property, the optical performances of the PVA/Y-CDs composite was investigated as shown in Fig. 6 . The PVA/Y-CDs composite film with thickness of 0.2 mm demonstrated relatively low transmittance compared with pure PVA film (Fig. 6a), which was attributed to the incorporation of brunet CD particles in PVA matrix and the light absorption behavior of Y-CDs. As expected, because the single fluorescence source was supplied by Y-CDs, the as-prepared Y-CDs/CDs composite film also demonstrated excitation-independent PL behavior as similar to the pristine Y-CDs (Fig. 6b). Clearly, the emission peak of the nanocomposite was located at about 533 nm, showing blue-shift phenomenon in comparison with Y-CDs aqueous solution, as seen in Fig. 6c. The underlying reason for the shift of emission peak could be related to the fact that the formation of hydrogen bonds between Y-CDs and PVA matrix changed the electron transition channels within Y-CDs. Despite showing blue-shifted phenomenon, the as-prepared composite film with strong yellow-green fluorescence still has great advantage in biomedical filed once being bestowed with water-responsive shape memory property.

Further investigation on the shape recovery performance of PVA/Y-CDs composite was performed, as shown in Fig. 7 a. A quatrefoil shaped sample was prepared and deformed through folding. It is observed clearly that all petals of the closed flower shape were able to bloom by the water-induced recovery process within a short duration of around 60 s. This behavior demonstrated that the Y-CDs-contained PVA film exhibited outstanding water-induced shape memory property. More importantly, benefiting from the bright yellow-green fluorescence arising from Y-CDs, the whole recovery process can be traced under a commercial UV lamp (365 nm excitation). To further confirm the contribution of the Y-CDs toward shape recovery performance, the water-induced shape memory effect of PVA/Y-CDs was also quantitatively assessed by a classical bending-recovery test under different pH conditions. As presented in Fig. 7b, all the three composite specimens displayed superior water-induced shape memory properties with recovery ratio over 92%, while the response rate was dependent on the water pH heavily. It can be clearly seen that the original straight sample can be recovered within 60 s upon immersing in water with pH value of 3, when the water pH changed to 7, the duration of complete recovery of PVA/Y-CDs composite was 120 s, with further increasing pH value to 11, the deformed strip recovered to its original shape slowly after been immersed in water at room temperature for about 180 s. However, as we can see, the shape recovery of pure PVA film after deformation met tremendous obstacle, the recovery ratio was only 29.3% even after being immersed in water (pH = 7) for 180 s (Fig. 7c). The above-mentioned results clearly suggest that the PVA/Y-CDs nanocomposite was advantageous over the pure PVA in terms of shape memory effect. In particular, the visual images in Fig. 7d and e also demonstrated that the recovery process of PVA/Y-CDs composite strip can be monitored under UV light of 365 nm with respect to the straight sample of pure PVA. Hence, the incorporation of Y-CDs in PVA matrix not only contributed more to the shape memory property, but also offered excellent performance in the visualization of shape recovery process when the SMPs was used in a dark environment.

For better understanding the mechanism of superior shape recovery in PVA/Y-CDs SMPs actuated by water, a systematic investigation on the effect of Y-CDs toward PVA was carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 a, an obvious increase of storage modulus obtained from DMA was observed, from 4195 MPa for pure PVA to 5836 MPa for PVA/Y-CDs composite. Besides, the glass transition temperature (T g), which is defined as the peak position of maximum tan δ, showed similar tendency, i.e., the T g of PVA-based SMPs markedly increased from 41.5 °C to 49.0 °C with the introduction of Y-CDs (Fig. 8b). The above experimental results are in good agreement with pioneer’s investigation on the thermomechanical properties of PVA/CDs composite [29], and also evidence that the Y-CDs as functionalized fillers induced the formation of additional crosslinking points in PVA matrix via interfacial interactions. The difference between pure PVA and PVA/Y-CDs composite in crystallization was also examined by XRD analysis in Fig. 8c. The 2θ position of the peak in the X-ray scan for the nanocomposite was almost same as that of pure PVA, meaning that the Y-CDs can only participated in the formation of physical crosslinking network. Note that the prepared PVA/Y-CDs composite had a relatively low diffraction intensity compared with pure PVA film, the decreased PVA crystallinity in the nanocomposite indicated that the chain mobility of PVA was higher in the nanocomposite than in pure PVA film, such performance could be highly related to the Y-CDs-induced extra crosslinking points in PVA polymer. Taking into consideration that the nanosized Y-CDs possessed a great number of amino groups as well as large surface area, it was easy to form hydrogen bonding interactions between Y-CDs and PVA matrix, so that provided abundant extra crosslinking points in the resultant nanocomposite. FTIR spectra were used to identify the hydrogen bonding interactions, as displayed in Fig. 8d. It is apparent that pure PVA showed typical stretching vibration of O–H centering at 3287 cm−1; however, with the incorporation of Y-CDs, the absorption peak of O–H stretching vibration shifted from 3287 to 3253 cm−1, the blueshift in FTIR spectra fully revealed strong hydrogen bonding interaction between O–H in PVA and N–H on Y-CDs surfaces. As another critical evidence, the PVA/Y-CDs composite possessed a tensile strength of 57.4 MPa, which was 22.9% increase compared with the 46.7 MPa of the pure PVA (Fig. S7). The improved mechanical performance obtained in the CDs-contained nanocomposite was more likely due to the intense hydrogen bonding interactions between Y-CDs and PVA chains. According to the previous researches concerning water responsive PVA-based SMPs [28], we proposed an underlying mechanism to clarify the improved shape memory effect of PVA/Y-CDs composite. Since multiple hydrophilic groups in the PVA/Y-CDs nanocomposite contributed to the affinity toward water molecules, the competition of water-induced plasticity and Y-CDs-induced hydrogen bonding interactions would occur within the structure of nanocomposite, such behavior weakened the H-bond based physical crosslinking effect resulting from immobilized Y-CDs, and the plastication from water penetration led to the active motion of soft segment and then the shape recovery was happened. In addition, since the dissociation of hydrogen bonds which were formed between amino groups and hydroxyl groups became easy as the water pH decreasing, the shape recovery ratio was highly dependent on the acid base environment.

In summary, with the assistance of microwave irradiation, yellow emissive Y-CDs with a high quantum yield of 62.8% were facilely synthesized using resorcinol and oPD as starting materials in HCl solution. To probe the underlying reason for the long-wavelength emission, G-CDs with strong green fluorescence were also prepared following the same synthesis strategy except using EDA as N-dopant to replace oPD. The systematic investigations on the emission behavior, morphological structure, chemical composition indicated that the large conjugated sp2-domain and graphitic N within the Y-CDs structure dominated their long-wavelength emission. Furthermore, the considerable QY determined from the synthesized CDs was mainly correlated with the complete carbon framework as well as abundant N-containing groups within CDs, because the reduction of carbon vacancy in carbogenic core, as well as the enriched electron density could promote more effective radiative recombination from electron-hole pair. Since the Y-CDs can introduce the formation H-bond based physical crosslinking networks within PVA matrix, the resultant PVA/Y-CDs composite showed more excellent water-induced shape memory effect over pure PVA. Furthermore, the shape recovery ratio of the nanocomposite showed increasing tendency as the water pH increased, demonstrating that the tunable responsiveness of the Y-CDs-contained nanocomposite can be achieved when using water as stimuli. More importantly, labelling the PVA-based SMP with long-wavelength emission was beneficial to monitoring shape recovery in dark environment, which will provide great possibilities for its utilization in diverse biological fields, such as smart biosensor, stimuli-responsive drug-release system and novel medical devices.

Pei Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ziqi Zhu: Investigation. Xinghui Li: Investigation. Tao Zhang: Investigation. Wei Zhang: Investigation. Minzhi Chen: Conceptualization. Xiaoyan Zhou: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_839), the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.
